metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan which component sets `acknowledged=true`, under what condition (e.g. 'this loop sets acknowledged=true only after all label removals in the stack have returned 200/404/409'), and which story owns that write-back if it is not this story.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field in the outcome record has an undefined write-back owner: the plan says 'on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives label removal to completion' but never specifies what sets acknowledged=true, when, or which story is responsible — an executor implementing this story cannot complete AC5 without guessing.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC5 (Idempotent ordering under retries/restart)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Outcome record section: 'The auto-lander loop sets `acknowledged = true` immediately after all label-removal calls for the stack have completed (200, 404, or 409 treated as success). S4/S9 only read this field; they do not write it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `stack_id` explicitly: specify its derivation rule (e.g., root/tip change_id, relation-chain identifier), confirm it is stable across re-runs of the same stack, and reconcile it with S2's wipChain schema so S4/S9 can consume the same key.
findings.finding: `stack_id` is used as the upsert key for the outcome record and is referenced as the cross-story SSOT consumed by S4 and S9, but is never defined anywhere in the plan. S2's wipChain schema contains no `stack_id` field. Without a definition, neither its derivation rule (tip change_id? root change_id? hash of chain_member_ids[]?) nor its stability guarantee across re-runs is knowable, breaking the idempotency and cross-story consumption mechanism.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC5 ('upsert-keyed on `stack_id`'); Dependencies (S2 wipChain schema)
findings.suggested_fix: Define stack_id as the Gerrit change number of the stack's root (bottom-most) change in the relation chain, sourced from wipChain.chain_member_ids[0]. This is stable: the root never changes identity even as the chain is rebased, and it is derivable from S2's existing schema without a new concept.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'append-only' vs 'upsert-keyed overwrite' contradiction in the Outcome record section by picking one storage semantic and justifying it — either adopt rebar's append-only+projection pattern (consistent with the codebase) or explicitly justify the mutable-upsert choice by comparing it against alternatives and documenting why a flat-file upsert is preferred over append-only log or SQLite.
findings.finding: The 'auto-lander state dir' outcome record is described as both 'append-only JSON' and 'upsert-keyed on stack_id (re-run overwrites prior record)' — these are contradictory storage semantics. The plan introduces a novel persistence pattern (mutable upsert file outside rebar's event-sourced store) with no prior-art consideration of alternatives (append-only + projection, SQLite, in-memory + restart-recovery log) and no justification for this specific choice.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'The bot writes an append-only JSON record … upsert-keyed on stack_id (a re-run of the same stack overwrites its prior record) — NOT one blind append per attempt.'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'append-only' from the description (it is wrong for an upsert). Add a one-sentence justification: e.g., 'A single mutable JSON file per stack_id is chosen over an append-only log because the record is the authoritative current state (not a history), and S4/S9 need only the latest outcome without replay; recovery from a partial write is handled by the idempotent restart path (unacknowledged record re-drives label removal).' If append semantics are actually intended, switch to a log with a projection query keyed on stack_id.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a write-protocol for `acknowledged` co-located with the schema: specify the initial value (e.g. `false`), the actor that sets it `true` (the lander loop itself, after confirming all label removals succeeded), and that S4/S9 are read-only consumers of this field.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field in the outcome record schema has no write-protocol specified: the plan never states who sets it, what the initial value is, what event or actor transitions it from false→true, or where that transition code lives. The only reference is in the restart AC ('an unacknowledged record re-drives'), which assumes the field exists and has a known transition point, but the decision rule for writing it is absent from the schema definition.
findings.location: Outcome record section (schema definition) and AC-5 (idempotent ordering under retries/restart)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Outcome record section, add: '`acknowledged` is written exclusively by the lander loop; it is set `false` on record creation and flipped to `true` only after every `Autosubmit` removal for the stack has received a 2xx or no-op 404/409. S4 and S9 are read-only consumers and MUST NOT write this field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `wipChain.phase` transition after each failure path: state which phase value (e.g. `idle` or a new `failed` phase added to S2's enum) the `wipChain` enters after recording the outcome and completing label removal, so the loop's next iteration correctly selects a new stack.
findings.finding: The `wipChain` phase after a failure event is never specified. S2 defines `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle}`. S3 adds CI-fail and 409-conflict branches to S2's loop but does not state which phase the `wipChain` enters after the outcome is recorded — whether it transitions to `idle`, is cleared, or requires a new phase value like `failed`. Without this, the executor cannot determine S2's loop's next-iteration behavior after S3's failure handling completes.
findings.location: AC-1 (CI-fail path), AC-2 (rebase-conflict path); cross-ref S2 wipChain schema (phase field)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC-1 and AC-2: 'After label removal completes, set `wipChain.phase = idle` and clear `wipChain.change_id` so the next loop iteration re-enters the `selecting` phase.' If a new phase value is needed, amend S2's `phase` enum explicitly.

findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field's lifecycle is ambiguous: the plan states S3 writes it into the outcome record and that 'an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion on restart', but it never specifies (a) what value S3 assigns on first write (`false`? omitted?), (b) what constitutes acknowledgment (label removal fully applied? S4/S9 consumption?), and (c) which component sets it to `true`. An executor must guess whether S3 owns both the write and the flip, or whether S4/S9 flip it after consuming the record.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC5 ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Outcome record section: '`acknowledged` (bool, default `false`): set to `true` by S3 once every label-removal in the stack has completed (2xx/404/409). S4 and S9 read but never write this field; they treat any record regardless of `acknowledged` value as authoritative for the outcome type.'

findings.finding: The `stack_id` field is used as the upsert key throughout the schema and consumed by S4 and S9, but its derivation rule is never defined — no formula (e.g., derived from tip change id, hash of change_ids[], Gerrit topic name) is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Outcome record section: '{stack_id, change_ids[], outcome ∈ {needs_rebase, ci_failed}, failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}' and AC 5 upsert-key clause

findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field is declared in the record schema and referenced in AC5's restart-recovery rule ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion'), but no processing protocol specifies what sets `acknowledged` to true, who sets it, and at what point in the workflow.
findings.location: Outcome record section (field `acknowledged`) and AC5 idempotent-ordering bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Outcome record section: '`acknowledged` is set to `true` by S3 only, after all `Autosubmit` label-removal calls for every `change_ids[]` entry have returned 200, 404, or 409. S4 and S9 treat `acknowledged=false` as "removal may be incomplete" and MUST NOT set this field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the idempotency AC) that specifies when `acknowledged` is set to `true` — e.g., after all `Autosubmit` label-removal calls complete without error — so the write path of this schema field is covered.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field is described in the Outcome record schema and its read path is covered by the idempotency AC ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion'), but no AC specifies when or how `acknowledged` is set to `true` — its write path is a described deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Outcome record section (field `acknowledged`) + Idempotency AC
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the idempotency AC: '...once all label removals complete (or return 404/409 no-ops), the record is updated to `acknowledged: true`; subsequent restarts skip label removal for acknowledged stacks.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add auth preconditions to the S3 plan: name the `autolander-bot` HTTP token SSM parameter (e.g. `/rebar/prod/autolander-bot-token`), the environment variable the service reads it from, and reference the provisioning step (a new `service-user.sh`-equivalent or an extension of S1's `setup-project.sh`) so the Gerrit label-removal calls authenticate correctly as `autolander-bot`.
findings.finding: The plan makes multiple Gerrit REST calls on behalf of `autolander-bot` (label removal via `POST /revisions/{rev}/review`, at minimum one call per stack member) but states no auth/HTTPS preconditions: which HTTP token is used, how it is provisioned, where it is stored (SSM path), and how it is injected into the autolander service at runtime. The `Autosubmit` label vote ACL (from S1) grants `label-Autosubmit = -1..+1` only to `Autosubmit Voters` (containing `autolander-bot`) + Administrators. The existing review-bot uses `BOT_USER:GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN` for `rebar-review-bot` — a separate identity. The autolander service needs a distinct `autolander-bot` HTTP token, provisioned and rotatable, but the plan is entirely silent on this.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail item ('remove Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack'), Rebase-conflict item ('remove Autosubmit from all changes in the stack'), Idempotent ordering item ('label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Auth preconditions' section: 'The autolander service authenticates to Gerrit REST as `autolander-bot` using HTTP Basic auth (`autolander-bot:<AUTOLANDER_BOT_TOKEN>`). The token is provisioned by `infra/gerrit/autolander-service-user.sh` (new, mirrors `service-user.sh`) and stored at SSM path `/rebar/prod/autolander-bot-token`, injected as `AUTOLANDER_BOT_TOKEN` at runtime. S1 must merge and provision the `autolander-bot` Gerrit account before S3 can be deployed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing section that specifies at minimum: (a) a unit/integration test for the CI-fail path (Verified -1 triggers record + all-stack Autosubmit removal), (b) a test for the 409/needs_rebase path, (c) a test for idempotent restart re-drive (simulate crash after record write, before label removal; verify re-run completes removal), and (d) a test that label-removal 404/409 is treated as a no-op.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new behavioral paths — CI-fail detection, 409/rebase-conflict handling, bulk Autosubmit removal, and idempotent restart re-drive — but specifies no testing approach whatsoever. There are no unit tests, integration tests, or acceptance-test scenarios named anywhere in the plan. The failure paths (CI-fail, 409) are themselves the new user-facing flows, and they have zero test coverage specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets); Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a '## Testing' section listing: (1) a parametrized test covering both outcome types (ci_failed / needs_rebase) asserting the outcome record is written before any Gerrit label call; (2) a test that after a simulated mid-run restart the unacknowledged record causes label removal to be retried; (3) a test that a stack with N changes has Autosubmit removed from all N (not just the failing one); (4) a test that allow_conflicts is never passed to POST /rebase.

findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new user-facing failure flows (CI-fail path, 409 rebase-conflict path, idempotent restart/recovery path, stack-atomic Autosubmit removal) but contains zero testing specification — no unit, integration, or end-to-end test strategy is described anywhere. The ACs state desired behavior but do not constitute a test plan. Critically, the sequencing invariant 'outcome recorded BEFORE Autosubmit removal' and the partial-failure scenario (label removal fails mid-stack, then service restarts) have no adversarial test coverage called out.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all ACs); 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section to the plan with: (1) Unit tests: outcome record upsert-on-same-stack_id, label-removal 404/409 treated as no-op, CI-fail and 409 routing logic. (2) Integration test (Gerrit sandbox or recorded-replay): POST /rebase 409 → outcome written before any label call → all Autosubmit labels removed → restart with unacknowledged record → labels removed again idempotently. (3) Fault-injection test: simulate label-removal failure on change N-of-M mid-stack, assert restart completes remaining removals without duplicating the outcome record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete filesystem path for the outcome-record state directory (e.g. `infra/autolander/state/`) so S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) can locate the file without out-of-band coordination.
findings.finding: The plan's primary edit target (`infra/autolander/loop.py` and the `infra/autolander/` state dir) does not exist in the repo — S2 is pre-manifest — so the implementer has no concrete filesystem path for the outcome-record state directory. The plan says 'auto-lander state dir (project-infra state)' but never specifies the actual path (e.g. `infra/autolander/state/` or similar), which is load-bearing for S4 and S9 to consume it.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'the auto-lander state dir (project-infra state, NOT a rebar event)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'State-dir path' sub-section under Outcome record, e.g.: 'Written to `infra/autolander/state/<stack_id>.json`; S4 and S9 read from the same path.' This makes the cross-story contract explicit and grounded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (or test reference) for each AC — e.g. for AC5: `jq -e 'length == 1' <(jq -s '.' state-dir/stacks/*.json | jq '[.[] | select(.stack_id=="X")]')` after two runs on the same stack; for AC1/AC2: grep/jq assertions on the outcome file plus a Gerrit API call to confirm label absent.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command; every AC describes behavior but provides zero executable check that produces evidence on success — a reviewer or CI cannot confirm done-ness without ad-hoc investigation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Verification' section listing per-AC checks: (AC1) after injecting a Verified -1 event, assert `jq -e '.outcome=="ci_failed"' <state-file>` and `gerrit query --format json change:X | jq -e '.labels.Autosubmit == null'`; (AC2) after mock-returning 409 from POST /rebase, same file + label checks; (AC5) run the handler twice for the same stack_id, then assert `jq -e '. | length == 1' <(jq -s '.' state-dir/*.json)`; (AC4) assert no patchset with conflict markers exists via `git log --oneline` on the fetched ref.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the 409-handling AC to specify that the autolander inspects the response body (e.g., checks for conflict-marker language vs. 'already up to date') before routing to `needs_rebase`, or add a spike/fixture to empirically confirm which 409 sub-cases `POST /rebase` can return and how to distinguish them.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a `POST /rebase` 409 unambiguously signals a rebase conflict and that this is the sole trigger for the `needs_rebase` outcome path. However, Gerrit's `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` can return HTTP 409 for reasons other than a merge conflict — notably 'Change is already up to date' (change is already on current tip) and 'Cannot rebase: New patch set is not uploaded yet'. No in-repo empirical probe or documentation citation disambiguates these 409 sub-cases, leaving open whether non-conflict 409s will incorrectly route to `needs_rebase` and strip `Autosubmit`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Rebase-conflict: on POST /rebase 409, record a needs_rebase outcome'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly anchor 'all changes in the stack' for label removal to `wipChain.chain_member_ids[]` (the snapshot captured at wip-selection time), not a live re-query at failure time, and state this in the AC or Outcome record section.
findings.finding: The plan's 'remove Autosubmit from all changes in the stack' does not specify whether membership is taken from the wipChain.chain_member_ids[] snapshot (frozen at wip time) or from a live re-query of the Gerrit relation chain at failure time. The two can diverge if the relation chain was modified concurrently. Using a live re-query may over-remove (hitting changes that were never in the wip run); using a stale snapshot may under-remove (missing changes added after wip selection). The membership source is load-bearing for the 'never orphan a descendant' invariant.
findings.location: AC1 ('remove Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack'); AC2 ('remove Autosubmit from all changes in the stack'); AC3 ('remove Autosubmit from every change in it')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1/AC2: 'Membership for removal is defined as wipChain.chain_member_ids[] — the snapshot taken at selection time — not a live re-query of the relation chain.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit population rule for `failing_change_id?`: specify for each outcome value (`ci_failed`, `needs_rebase`) whether the field is set (and to what) or explicitly null.
findings.finding: The `failing_change_id?` field in the outcome record schema has no decision rule for when it is populated versus left null. The schema marks it optional (`?`) but provides no protocol for which failure mode sets it and which omits it, leaving the executor to infer intent for both `ci_failed` and `needs_rebase` paths.
findings.location: Outcome record section — `failing_change_id?` field
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Outcome record section: '`failing_change_id` is set to the change that received `Verified -1` for `ci_failed`; set to the change whose `POST /rebase` returned 409 for `needs_rebase`. Never null when the triggering change is known.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the population rule for `tested_sha` in each failure path: for `ci_failed` it is the post-rebase SHA on which CI ran (from `wipChain.tested_sha`); for `needs_rebase` (409 before any rebase succeeds) it should be explicitly `null` or the SHA of the tip change before the attempted rebase.
findings.finding: The `tested_sha` field in the outcome record has no rule specifying which SHA is captured at failure time. For CI-fail, the loop has already done a rebase and the `tested_sha` is the post-rebase SHA on which CI ran — but the plan does not state this. For a rebase-conflict (409), no rebased SHA exists yet, so it would be the pre-attempt SHA — also unstated. An executor producing the record must guess.
findings.location: Outcome record section — `tested_sha` field; AC-1 and AC-2

findings.finding: The plan specifies WHAT triggers CI-fail detection ('post-rebase Verified -1') but never specifies HOW the loop observes this event. The codebase has a webhook receiver (ADR-0007) and a REST vote path (ADR-0023), but neither the event subscription mechanism nor the correlation of a Verified -1 vote back to the correct patchset/stack is described. Without this, an implementer may build a polling loop (introducing a detection window) or may incorrectly correlate a -1 on the pre-rebase patchset rather than the post-rebase one, leaving the stack in an inconsistent state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail bullet ('on a post-rebase Verified -1, record a ci_failed outcome')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mechanism note: 'The loop subscribes to Gerrit label-vote events (or polls GET /changes/{id}?o=DETAILED_LABELS after each rebase) and checks Verified on the current revision (current_revision field) only — a -1 on any prior patchset is ignored. The current_revision is obtained from the POST /rebase response body.'

findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field is present in the outcome record schema but neither who sets it nor when records are deleted/cleaned up is defined. This means records accumulate indefinitely (retirement path is missing) and the 'on restart re-drives unacknowledged record' semantics are ambiguous about what acknowledgement means and who performs it.
findings.location: Outcome record section — '{...acknowledged}'; Idempotent ordering AC

findings.finding: The `failing_change_id?` field is marked optional in the schema but no decision rule specifies when it is populated versus left null — specifically, whether the `needs_rebase` (409) path always has a failing change id and the `ci_failed` path may or may not.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'failing_change_id?' field

findings.finding: The `tested_sha` field is included in the record schema but the plan never defines what SHA value it holds (e.g., the patchset SHA before rebase, the SHA after rebase submitted to CI, the current tip SHA) nor how downstream consumers S4/S9 are expected to use it.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'tested_sha' field

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full lifecycle of the `acknowledged` field: which story/component sets it (S4? S9?), what triggers its being set, and what the cleanup/deletion policy is for acknowledged records (e.g., delete after N days or after successful re-land).
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field is declared in the outcome record schema but its lifecycle is undefined in this plan: who sets it to true, when, and what triggers cleanup of records once acknowledged. Without a RETIRE path, outcome records accumulate indefinitely per stack.
findings.location: Outcome record section — '{..., acknowledged}'; 'on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC (or an explicit rationale for deferral to a named story) that exercises the CI-fail and 409 paths end-to-end against a real or staging Gerrit instance, confirming label removal and outcome-file contents with a live API call.
findings.finding: The plan exercises new code paths (CI-fail handler, 409 handler) with no AC that runs them end-to-end against a live/real Gerrit instance; all described verification is implicitly unit/mock-level, meaning the live Gerrit label-removal and rebase API boundary is never exercised as a proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a retry/backoff policy (e.g., 3 retries with exponential backoff) for transient Gerrit API errors (5xx, network timeout) on both `POST /rebase` and label-removal calls, distinct from the permanent-failure (409 for rebase) routing.
findings.finding: No retry/backoff is specified for transient Gerrit API failures (5xx, network timeout) on either `POST /rebase` or label-removal calls. A single transient error could incorrectly trigger the failure path or leave Autosubmit labels un-removed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail and Rebase-conflict bullets; 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' AC line
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish permanent errors (409 on rebase → needs_rebase path) from transient errors (5xx/network → retry with exponential backoff up to N attempts, then surface as a loop-level error without recording a failure outcome). For label removal, retry 5xx up to N times before logging and continuing (label removal failure should not block outcome recording).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that outcome records are written atomically (e.g., write to a temp file then rename into place) so a crash mid-write cannot corrupt the record and the restart-recovery path remains reliable.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify atomic file-write semantics for the outcome record. A crash between an in-progress write and fsync could corrupt the JSON file, making the record unreadable on restart and violating the 'recovery without data loss' requirement.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'The bot writes an append-only JSON record per failure to the auto-lander state dir'
findings.suggested_fix: Write to `<stack_id>.tmp.json`, fsync, then rename to `<stack_id>.json`. On startup, any `.tmp` file without a corresponding committed file is discarded (treated as not-written).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one live/end-to-end acceptance criterion (or a named integration-test target against a real or semi-real Gerrit sandbox) that exercises the 409→no-op label-removal path and the restart re-drive path against the actual Gerrit API boundary, or document an explicit rationale why the mock is production-faithful (e.g., deterministic HTTP contract tested elsewhere).
findings.finding: The AC for idempotent ordering and restart re-drive ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion') involves live Gerrit API calls (POST /rebase, label-removal endpoints). The plan gives no indication that these paths will be exercised end-to-end; if satisfied only with mocked/stubbed Gerrit responses, the proxy-validation anti-pattern applies — the suite goes green while the live Gerrit boundary is never exercised as it will be in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' bullet; 'Never allow_conflicts' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require atomic write semantics for the outcome JSON file: specify write-to-tempfile-then-os.rename (POSIX atomic) so a crash mid-write always leaves either the old valid record or the new valid record, never a corrupt intermediate.
findings.finding: The plan specifies an 'upsert' (overwrite on stack_id) to the JSON outcome file, but does not require atomic write semantics. A plain file write is NOT atomic: a process crash or OOM mid-write leaves a corrupt/partial JSON file. Since the entire restart-recovery guarantee depends on 'on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives label removal,' a corrupted file means neither recovery nor detection of the prior failure — permanently breaking the 'never partial-land' invariant.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC5 ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion')
findings.suggested_fix: Write the new record to a sibling tempfile (e.g., `<stack_id>.json.tmp`) then `os.replace()` it to `<stack_id>.json`. `os.replace()` is atomic on Linux (same filesystem) and guarantees no partial-write is ever visible to readers or restart-recovery logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify atomic write semantics for the JSON outcome record (e.g., write to a `.tmp` sibling file then `rename(2)` into place) so a crash mid-write leaves either the prior valid record or the new one — never a corrupt intermediate state.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify atomic write semantics for the JSON outcome record. A crash or OOM mid-write can leave a partial/corrupt JSON file on disk — neither the previous valid record nor the new one — breaking the idempotency guarantee ('upsert-keyed on stack_id') and potentially causing S4 and S9 consumers to fail or misread the outcome on the next restart.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Write to `<stack_id>.outcome.tmp` first, fsync, then `rename` to `<stack_id>.outcome.json`. On startup, delete any orphaned `.tmp` files before reading records. This makes every write atomic on POSIX filesystems.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state: either add a `schema_version` field to the record and specify how old readers handle unknown versions, or explicitly state that rollback requires pruning/migrating existing records and document that procedure.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema state of existing outcome records after a service rollback. If the bot is deployed, writes records in the new format, then is rolled back to a prior version that does not understand one or more fields (or the file-naming convention), those records remain on disk and may be misread or silently ignored by the old code — potentially re-driving Autosubmit removal incorrectly or not at all.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Dependencies section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a design-doc section (ADR or CLAUDE.md entry) capturing the outcome-record schema, its upsert-keyed semantics, the 'never partial-land' invariant, and the S3→S4/S9 contract so downstream implementers have a single authoritative reference.
findings.finding: The outcome-record schema and the 'stack → owner hand-back' contract are a novel architectural decision shared across S3, S4, and S9, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is planned to capture it.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — 'Stack → owner' AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR or CLAUDE.md block titled 'Auto-lander outcome record contract' that includes: (1) the canonical JSON schema with field semantics, (2) the upsert-keyed-on-stack_id rationale (re-run overwrites prior record), (3) the 'never partial-land / never evict from within stack' invariant, and (4) the list of consumers (S4 land command, S9 batch failure) and their read contract (always read the latest record for a stack_id).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: at minimum, log a structured event (stack_id, outcome, failing_change_id, timestamp) on each handback, and specify a metric or alert for sustained failure rates so operators can detect systemic issues.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the failure/handback events — no metrics, logs, or alerts — so operators cannot detect stacks silently accumulating in the failed state or diagnose why the failure path was triggered.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria overall

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency-safety mechanism for the stack_id upsert (e.g., file-lock per stack_id, CAS via temp-rename, or single-writer guarantee from the loop design) to prevent lost updates under concurrent processing.
findings.finding: The plan does not address concurrent-write safety on the stack_id upsert key. If two loop ticks or bot instances process the same stack simultaneously, both could read-then-overwrite the record in a non-atomic way, causing a lost update or corrupted record.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'upsert-keyed on stack_id (a re-run of the same stack overwrites its prior record)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the autolander's Gerrit label-removal calls will reuse `src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.GerritClient` (or a shared extract) rather than reimplementing Gerrit auth/XSSI/error-handling — and state this explicitly in the plan or file-impact list.
findings.finding: The plan requires authenticated Gerrit REST calls to remove the Autosubmit label from each stack member (POST /revisions/{rev}/review), but does not specify whether the autolander service will reuse the existing production GerritClient (src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.py) or reimplement Gerrit auth, XSSI-stripping, and error handling — the latter would be NIH duplication of existing codebase functionality.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail and Rebase-conflict items; AC 'Idempotent ordering' (label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a file-impact entry such as `infra/autolander/gerrit.py` (thin wrapper re-exporting or delegating to `GerritClient`), or note that `review_bot.gerrit_client` will be imported directly, so the 404/409 no-op behavior is covered by the existing `GerritError.status` field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that S3's plan is updated to explicitly name the expected S2 output artifact path (`infra/autolander/loop.py` or equivalent) and add a gate condition confirming that artifact exists before S3 implementation begins, so the 'adds failure branches to S2's loop' assertion is grounded at implementation time.
findings.finding: The plan asserts S3 'adds the failure/edge branches to S2's loop and consumes its wipChain + stack-selection', implying infra/autolander/loop.py (S2's sole file_impact) is an available substrate to extend — but that file and the entire infra/autolander/ directory do not exist in the repo (S2 is open/pre-manifest). The plan cannot be implemented as written if S2 slips or is restructured, and the assertion that S3 is a branch-addition to an existing loop is unverifiable against current code.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'this story adds the failure/edge branches to S2's loop and consumes its wipChain + stack-selection'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a precondition note: 'S3 implementation begins only after S2 merges infra/autolander/loop.py; if S2's file layout changes, update the S3 integration point accordingly.' This makes the dependency explicit and surfaces any S2 restructuring immediately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ticket IDs or aliases for S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) wherever they are named as outcome-record consumers, so the dependency is verifiable and reciprocal.
findings.finding: S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) are named as consumers of the outcome record but are not linked to ticket IDs or aliases. Without resolvable references, there is no way to verify that those stories' plans actually declare the reciprocal dependency on S3's record schema, leaving the consumer enumeration unverified.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this record as the authoritative outcome.'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the S2 pattern: e.g. 'S4 (land command, <alias>, <ticket-id>) and S9 (batch failure, <alias>, <ticket-id>) consume this record.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the file-based JSON outcome store (e.g., 'chosen because the service is single-instance, the schema must carry structured outcome fields for S4/S9 agent contract consumption, and a plain file avoids adding a database dependency to project-infra').
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing a file-based JSON state store over structurally different alternatives. The criterion requires the plan to justify WHY the chosen approach fits — its absence means an implementer cannot validate the design decision or know when to deviate.
findings.location: Outcome record section ('The bot writes an append-only JSON record... to the auto-lander state dir')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the Why or Scope section explaining why the 'whole stack returns as a unit' policy was chosen over partial-stack isolation (e.g., bisect to the failing change), grounding it in the agent-ownership model or referencing how established commit-queue tools (Bors, Mergify, Gerrit Submit Queue) handle this and why their approach does not apply here.
findings.finding: The 'whole stack returns to its owner on any failure' invariant is a novel architectural policy that crosses at least three stories (S3, S4, S9), but the plan does not demonstrate awareness of how established commit-queue tools handle partial-stack failures — specifically whether they evict just the failing change (splitting the stack) or fail the entire stack atomically — to justify the 'never evict from within a stack' decision.
findings.location: Why section: 'The whole stack returns to its owning agent to remediate'; AC 'Stack → owner': 'Never partial-land; never evict a change from within a stack.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: e.g., 'Partial eviction (isolating and landing only the passing prefix) is rejected because stacked changes are authored as a dependency chain — landing a prefix without the suffix orphans the suffix's review context and may produce an inconsistent intermediate state. The agent that authored the stack is the correct entity to decide how to remediate (reorder, split, or fix).' This grounds the invariant and prevents S9 from re-litigating it.

findings.finding: The `Autosubmit` label — the central mechanism S3 removes from changes on failure — does not exist anywhere in the codebase: absent from `infra/gerrit/project.config` (no label definition, no ACL entry), absent from `src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.py` (no removal operation), and absent from all of `src/` and `infra/`. S3's core ACs (CI-fail, rebase-conflict, stack hand-back, idempotent ordering) all depend on this label existing and being castable/removable, but no prerequisite story that creates it is enumerated.
findings.location: AC: CI-fail, Rebase-conflict, Stack → owner, Idempotent ordering; infra/gerrit/project.config
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / Edit Targets' section that lists: (1) `infra/gerrit/project.config` — add `[label "Autosubmit"]` block with `function = NoBlock`, `value = +1 Land this change`, `defaultValue = 0`, and an ACL entry granting the auto-lander service account `label-Autosubmit = -1..+1`; (2) `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` — re-run to push the updated config. These are hard prerequisites for every AC in S3.

findings.finding: The plan requires removing the `Autosubmit` label from changes whose vote may have been cast by a different account (the agent) than the auto-lander service. The existing `gerrit_client.py` only implements `POST /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review` to set its own votes. Removing another account's label vote in Gerrit requires a distinct endpoint (`DELETE /a/changes/{id}/reviewers/{account-id}/votes/{label-id}`) and the `removeReviewer` or `administrateServer` ACL permission — neither the endpoint nor the required permission is named anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail AC and Rebase-conflict AC: 'remove Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack'; Idempotent AC: 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `DELETE /a/changes/{id}/reviewers/{agent-account-id}/votes/Autosubmit` (Gerrit REST `DeleteVote` endpoint, available since Gerrit 2.14). Grant the auto-lander service account `removeReviewer` on `refs/heads/*` in `project.config`. Add this endpoint to `gerrit_client.py` as `delete_label_vote(change_id, account_id, label)` with 404/409 treated as no-op.

findings.finding: The `Autosubmit` label that S3 removes (as its primary failure-response action) is not confirmed to exist in `infra/gerrit/project.config` or anywhere else in the repo. If the label is not provisioned on the live Gerrit 3.14.1 instance, the `POST /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review` call with `labels:{Autosubmit:0}` will fail (400 / unrecognized label), making S3's entire CI-fail and rebase-conflict handlers non-functional.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail bullet ('remove `Autosubmit`') and Rebase-conflict bullet ('remove `Autosubmit` and record a `needs_rebase` outcome')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Dependencies / Prerequisites' section: 'Requires the `Autosubmit` label to be live in `refs/meta/config` (delivered by [prior story]). S3 cannot be implemented or tested until that label exists on the Gerrit instance.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
